You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for an Uber/Amazon-style senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD system prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, with all the prescribed sub-structure, emoji markers (🎬 🎙️ 💬 🗣️ 🧠 🤝 🆘 ⚠️ 🔀 ❓), and the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; IGNORE its old /Users/anshullkgarg/... paths — the real root is /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes, NO §1–§9-LLD structure. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code that ever appears: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in the API section, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers exactly — only the rounded results are surfaced in the markdown, never unrun code. REQUIRED SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, in this order — match the headings): ## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional) — 🎬 structure pitch opener; 3-5 functional flows "above the line" each with feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why it's hard; "Below the line (out of scope)" list; NFRs each with a talkable target (p99 ms, 9s) + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks without it; close with 🎙️ Script. TEXT ONLY. ## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions — each: spoken question, 🗣️ why I'm asking, ↔️ design fork (If YES.../If NO...), Assumption I'll proceed with; end with 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — a table: Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE the arithmetic by writing a capacity.py and running it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python capacity.py — paste the rounded results. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + its flip threshold. Plain-text math only (~, x, ÷, no LaTeX). End with a short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — per entity: name + what it owns, 🗣️ simple analogy, ⚠️ interviewer probe + one-line answer; 🎙️ Script. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+ why this verb), 2-4 key request fields (+ why; call out server-set fields), a concrete JSON request+response example, one key design decision (idempotency key etc.); security note (caller from JWT, never trust client ids/prices). 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: one-sentence framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted); 🗣️ key-terms gloss block; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action / why this component / state change / what could go wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing line; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" mermaid flowchart TD that is the reconciled union of all slices + 🎙️ Script + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (Entity | key fields with PK/shard key | chosen store | partition key | consistency); PART B a storage decision card per distinct store (what it is / why this — the access pattern that forces it / considered & rejected / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table. 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating Bad/Good/Great; each tier: ↩️ what previous got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach (mechanism) / ⚠️ what breaks (with concrete numbers) / 🔁 what forces the upgrade; Good & Great get a tier-specific mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision-forcing math + ✅ failure matrix. Each ends with 🎙️ Script + 🧠 "If they ask". Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, failure behavior. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s per path + human downtime + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (Component | what breaks | how it degrades gracefully — what USER SEES | recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define both in plain words first, then table per data class); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + why + rough monthly order-of-magnitude + the one biggest optimization). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards "🔀 Decision: [chose] vs [didn't]": what we chose & why / what we gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses (exact flip condition) / 🗣️ how to say it. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains (core algorithm/hardest part, failure, scale/hot-spot, consistency, security, cost, extensibility), 2-3 each, 12-18 total; each: ❓ question verbatim, the mechanism (2-3 sentences, terms glossed), 🗣️ in plain words, 💬 one-liner to say. End with a 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — get them right; HLD has MANY diagrams so this matters a lot): - Use ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT to "flowchart TD" for architecture/multi-path (NEVER flowchart LR for multi-path — it stretches off screen; LR only for a trivial 3-4 node linear chain). Use sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of these breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces { }. A semicolon ';' or colon ':' or parens inside a label/message BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks inside a node, never a raw newline. - Edge labels: short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| (text inside the pipes must itself avoid the breaking chars above — no ':' or ';' or parens inside it). - Same component = same node id/label across every diagram. Cap ~10-12 nodes per diagram. - Each §6.x slice gets its own diagram; §6 ends with one "Final (high-level)" union diagram; deep-dive Good/Great tiers get mechanism diagrams. Aim for 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . It must be LONG and comprehensive (the md must be > 20,000 chars; realistically 40-55K). Pure markdown — tables with real line breaks, fenced code only for the JSON API examples and mermaid. No LaTeX. Emoji markers as prescribed. - You MAY create a scratch workspace dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ to write capacity.py and run it with /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Do NOT write into /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles the JSON afterwards. Before finishing: (1) verify capacity.py actually runs and you used its real computed numbers; (2) self-check that all 11 section headings are present and the md is > 20K chars; (3) count your ```mermaid blocks and make sure each one's labels are free of the breaking characters listed above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 084c563c-9047-4d34-a2b8-18502316af3c TITLE: Design a Distributed Cache (Redis-style) PROBLEM BRIEF (this is what transcript[0] will say; frame §1 around it): A distributed in-memory key-value cache: get/put/expire, sharded across many nodes, replication for availability, eviction (LRU/LFU). Focus on partitioning (consistent hashing), replication + consistency, hot keys, and failure/failover. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these — this is what makes the session strong): consistent hashing for partitioning + rebalancing on node add/remove, replication (primary-replica) + consistency model, eviction policies, hot-key mitigation (replicate hot shard / client-side cache), cache stampede/thundering herd. Deep dives: consistent hashing + rebalancing, hot key, replication & failover, cache stampede. Assume an Amazon/Uber senior-SDE (SDE-3 / L5) bar: cost & operations are first-class, work backwards from the customer, decide and move. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/084c563c-9047-4d34-a2b8-18502316af3c_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first, write+run capacity.py for the numbers, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars = actual char count, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. For EACH endpoint write: 336 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 337 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 338 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 339 ``` 340 // Request 341 POST /v1/orders 342 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 343 344 // Response 200 345 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 346 ``` 347 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 348 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 349 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 350 351 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 352 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 353 ### 6.x "" 354 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 355 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 356 357 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 358 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 359 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 360 361 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 362 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 363 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 364 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 365 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 366 367 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 368 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 369 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 370 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 371 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 372 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 373 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 374 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 375 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 376 377 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 378 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 379 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 380 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 381 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 382 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 383 384 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 385 386 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 387 388 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 389 390 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 391 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 392 393 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 394 395 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 396 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 397 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 398 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 399 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 400 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 401 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 402 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 403 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 404 405 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 406 407 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 408 409 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 410 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 411 412 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 413 414 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 415 416 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 417 418 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 419 420 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 421 422 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 423 424 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 425 426 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 427 428 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 429 430 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 431 432 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 433 434 Each deep dive ends with: 435 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 436 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 437 438 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 439 440 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 441 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 442 443 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 444 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 445 446 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 447 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 448 449 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 450 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 451 452 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 453 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 454 455 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 456 457 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 458 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 459 460 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 461 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 462 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 463 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 464 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 465 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 466 467 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 468 469 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 470 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 471 472 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 473 474 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 475 476 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 477 478 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 479 480 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 481 482 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 483 484 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 485 486

content.text: I have a clear picture of the quality bar and structure. Now let me set up the workspace and write the capacity computation script.

input.description: Create workspace dir and verify venv python exists

content.text: Now let me write the capacity.py script with the math for a distributed cache.

input.content:
"""Capacity math for a distributed Redis-style cache.
All numbers are rounded to talkable figures in the markdown; this prints the raw chain.
"""

def r(x):
    """round to a talkable phrase"""
    return x

print("=" * 70)
print("DISTRIBUTED CACHE — CAPACITY")
print("=" * 70)

# ---- 1. Traffic ----
# Fleet of app services across a large company use this cache tier.
# Assume the cache fronts a primary datastore for a large product.
peak_ops = 10_000_000          # 10M ops/sec aggregate at peak (read+write)
read_frac = 0.90               # 90% reads (caches are read-heavy)
write_frac = 0.10
peak_reads = peak_ops * read_frac
peak_writes = peak_ops * write_frac
print(f"\n[Traffic]")
print(f"peak aggregate ops/s          = {peak_ops:,}")
print(f"peak reads/s  (90%)           = {peak_reads:,.0f}")
print(f"peak writes/s (10%)           = {peak_writes:,.0f}")

# ---- 2. Per-node ceiling and shard count ----
# A single Redis node single-threaded for commands tops out ~100K-150K ops/s
# for small GET/SET with pipelining off; pipelined can reach more but we size
# conservatively for p99 under mixed traffic.
node_ceiling = 100_000         # ops/s safe per primary node
shards_needed = peak_ops / node_ceiling
print(f"\n[Sharding]")
print(f"safe per-node ceiling ops/s   = {node_ceiling:,}")
print(f"shards needed (ops/ceiling)   = {peak_ops:,} / {node_ceiling:,} = {shards_needed:.0f}")
# add headroom 30% so no node runs hot, and round to power-friendly count
shards_with_headroom = shards_needed * 1.3
print(f"+30% headroom                 = {shards_with_headroom:.0f}")
shards = 128
print(f"chosen virtual-bucket-backed primary shards = {shards}")
per_shard_ops = peak_ops / shards
print(f"=> per-shard ops/s            = {peak_ops:,} / {shards} = {per_shard_ops:,.0f}")

# ---- 3. Replication / node count ----
repl_factor = 2                # 1 primary + 2 replicas per shard => RF total copies
copies = 1 + repl_factor
total_nodes = shards * copies
print(f"\n[Replication]")
print(f"replicas per shard            = {repl_factor} (1 primary + 2 replicas)")
print(f"total copies per shard        = {copies}")
print(f"total cache nodes             = {shards} x {copies} = {total_nodes}")

# ---- 4. Memory / dataset footprint ----
n_keys = 5_000_000_000         # 5B keys hot set
avg_val_bytes = 500            # avg value 500 bytes
avg_key_bytes = 50             # avg key 50 bytes
redis_overhead = 1.5           # ~50% overhead for dict, expiry, ptrs
bytes_per_entry = (avg_key_bytes + avg_val_bytes) * redis_overhead
raw_dataset = n_keys * bytes_per_entry
print(f"\n[Memory footprint]")
print(f"hot keys                      = {n_keys:,}")
print(f"avg entry bytes (k+v)x1.5 oh  = ({avg_key_bytes}+{avg_val_bytes}) x {redis_overhead} = {bytes_per_entry:.0f} B")
print(f"raw dataset (1 copy)          = {raw_dataset/1e12:.2f} TB")
dataset_replicated = raw_dataset * copies
print(f"x {copies} copies (replication) = {dataset_replicated/1e12:.2f} TB total RAM")
ram_per_node = dataset_replicated / total_nodes
print(f"RAM per node                  = {dataset_replicated/1e12:.2f}TB / {total_nodes} = {ram_per_node/1e9:.1f} GB")

# ---- 5. Hot key — the binding bottleneck ----
# Celebrity key: one key gets a disproportionate share of reads.
hot_key_share = 0.05           # one key = 5% of all reads during a spike
hot_key_qps = peak_reads * hot_key_share
print(f"\n[Hot key — binding bottleneck]")
print(f"hot key share of reads        = {hot_key_share*100:.0f}%")
print(f"hot key read qps              = {peak_reads:,.0f} x {hot_key_share} = {hot_key_qps:,.0f}")
print(f"single node ceiling           = {node_ceiling:,}")
print(f"=> hot key qps {hot_key_qps:,.0f} >> node ceiling {node_ceiling:,}  (overload by {hot_key_qps/node_ceiling:.0f}x)")
# Relief: replicate hot shard to N read replicas + client local cache
read_replicas_for_hot = hot_key_qps / node_ceiling
print(f"read replicas to serve hot key= {hot_key_qps:,.0f} / {node_ceiling:,} = {read_replicas_for_hot:.0f}")
# client-side cache absorbs e.g. 95% of repeat reads of the hot key
client_cache_hit = 0.95
residual_hot = hot_key_qps * (1 - client_cache_hit)
print(f"with client-side cache {client_cache_hit*100:.0f}% hit, residual = {residual_hot:,.0f} ops/s -> {residual_hot/node_ceiling:.1f} nodes")

# ---- 6. Cache stampede / thundering herd ----
# When a popular key expires, all concurrent requests miss and stampede the DB.
concurrent_on_miss = hot_key_qps         # everyone hitting that key in the miss window
db_ceiling = 5_000              # origin DB can do ~5K qps for this key class
print(f"\n[Cache stampede]")
print(f"requests in miss window       = {concurrent_on_miss:,.0f} (all hit DB)")
print(f"origin DB ceiling per key qps = {db_ceiling:,}")
print(f"=> stampede overloads DB by   = {concurrent_on_miss/db_ceiling:.0f}x  -> single-flight + early recompute")

# ---- 7. Rebalancing on node add/remove (consistent hashing) ----
# Naive hashing (mod N): adding 1 node remaps ~ (N-1)/N of keys.
N = shards
naive_remap = (N - 1) / N
print(f"\n[Rebalancing — consistent hashing]")
print(f"naive hash mod N remap on +1  = (N-1)/N = {naive_remap*100:.1f}% of keys move")
# consistent hashing: only ~1/N of keys move
ch_remap = 1 / (N + 1)
print(f"consistent hashing remap on +1= ~1/N = {ch_remap*100:.2f}% of keys move")
keys_moved_naive = n_keys * naive_remap
keys_moved_ch = n_keys * ch_remap
print(f"keys remapped naive           = {keys_moved_naive/1e9:.2f}B  (cold-cache disaster)")
print(f"keys remapped consistent-hash = {keys_moved_ch/1e6:.0f}M")

# ---- 8. Replica lag / consistency window ----
# Async replication: replica lag under load.
repl_lag_ms = 5                # typical intra-region async replica lag
print(f"\n[Consistency]")
print(f"async replica lag (intra-AZ)  = ~{repl_lag_ms} ms (stale-read window on replica reads)")

# ---- 9. Failover RTO ----
sentinel_detect_ms = 5000      # failure detection (down-after + quorum)
promote_ms = 1000              # promote replica to primary
rto = (sentinel_detect_ms + promote_ms) / 1000
print(f"\n[Failover]")
print(f"detect (down-after quorum) ms = {sentinel_detect_ms}")
print(f"promote replica ms            = {promote_ms}")
print(f"=> failover RTO               = ~{rto:.0f} s per shard")

# ---- 10. Cost order-of-magnitude ----

# Design a Distributed Cache (Redis-style)

> 🎙️ **Opener:** "We're building a distributed in-memory key-value cache — a Redis-style tier that sits in front of a slower datastore and serves `get`/`put`/`expire` at single-digit-millisecond latency. The interesting part isn't the hash map; it's making that hash map survive being spread across hundreds of machines: partitioning so any node can find any key, replication so a node dying doesn't lose data or drop traffic, eviction so memory never overflows, and surviving hot keys and cache stampedes without melting either the cache or the origin behind it."

---

## 1. Requirements (Functional + Non-Functional)

> 🎙️ **Structure pitch:** "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and the core entities, draw the high-level design one operation at a time, then go deep on the four parts that actually decide whether this works at scale — consistent hashing and rebalancing, hot keys, replication and failover, and cache stampede. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension: a single in-memory hash map is trivially fast but caps out on one machine's RAM and CPU. The whole job is to **spread that map across a fleet without losing the speed** — and to keep it correct and available while nodes are constantly being added, dying, and getting hammered by skewed traffic.

### Functional requirements (the core flows — "above the line")

**FR1 — GET a value by key.** A client should be able to ask for a key and get back the value (or a miss) in single-digit milliseconds.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You hand me a label, I hand you back what's stored under it — instantly — or tell you we don't have it."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the key could live on any of hundreds of nodes; the client (or a router) has to know *which* node owns it without asking a central directory on every read.

**FR2 — PUT (set) a key-value with optional TTL.** A client should be able to write a value under a key, optionally with a time-to-live after which it auto-expires.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "You give me a label and a value and say 'keep this for 60 seconds,' and I store it and forget it on time automatically."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** the write has to land on the key's owning node *and* be replicated so a node crash doesn't lose it — and we have to decide whether the client waits for replicas (slow, safe) or not (fast, lossy).

**FR3 — EXPIRE / TTL and eviction.** Keys must expire on their TTL, and when a node fills up its RAM, it must evict (LRU/LFU) to make room rather than crash.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Old or unused stuff gets thrown out automatically so we never run out of room — like a fridge that quietly tosses the oldest leftovers when it's full."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** tracking 'least recently used' precisely across millions of keys is itself memory- and CPU-expensive, so we approximate; and expiry of a *popular* key is what triggers the stampede deep dive.

**FR4 — Partitioning across nodes (consistent hashing).** Keys must be spread across many nodes, and adding or removing a node must move only a small slice of keys, not reshuffle everything.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "When we add a new machine to the pool, only a tiny fraction of the data has to move — the rest stays put — so the cache doesn't go cold."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** naive `hash(key) % N` remaps ~all keys when N changes — a cold-cache catastrophe that hammers the origin DB. Consistent hashing is the whole point.

**FR5 — Replication + failover.** Each shard's data must have copies on other nodes, and if the primary for a shard dies, a replica must be promoted automatically with seconds of downtime, not minutes.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Every piece of data lives on more than one machine, so if one dies, another instantly takes over and you barely notice."
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** promoting a replica requires agreeing *who's in charge now* without two nodes both thinking they're primary (split-brain) — that needs a coordinator and a quorum.

### Below the line (out of scope)

- Multi-key transactions / Lua scripting atomicity across shards (single-key only here).
- Durable persistence as a system-of-record (we're a cache; the DB behind us is the source of truth — we'll keep an AOF/snapshot for warm restart only).
- Secondary indexes / range queries / full-text search (this is a KV store, not a database).
- Cross-region active-active replication (we'll mention it; primary design is single-region multi-AZ).
- Pub/sub, streams, geo commands (Redis has them; not core to "a cache").

### Non-Functional requirements

**NFR1 — Latency: p99 < 1 ms on a cache HIT (intra-AZ), p99 < 5 ms including a client→router→node round trip.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "99 out of 100 lookups come back faster than you can blink — under a millisecond at the node."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** if the cache is slow, callers fall back to the database, the whole point of the cache evaporates, and the DB — which we sized assuming the cache absorbs 90% of reads — gets crushed.

**NFR2 — Availability: 99.99% on the read path ("four nines" ≈ 52 minutes of downtime per year).**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "The cache is essentially always up — under an hour of total outage across a whole year."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** every minute the cache is unavailable, the origin DB eats the full unshielded read load (10x–100x its provisioned capacity) and likely falls over too — a correlated outage.

**NFR3 — Scalability: linear horizontal scale to ~10M ops/s, elastic — add nodes without a maintenance window.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "Need more throughput? Add machines and it just goes faster, no downtime."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** traffic growth forces a forklift migration; or worse, a single node becomes the ceiling and we can't grow past it.

**NFR4 — Consistency: read-your-own-writes from the perspective of a single client path; bounded staleness (~single-digit ms) on replica reads.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If you write something then immediately read it back, you see your own write; other readers might see the old value for a few milliseconds."
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a user updates their profile, refreshes, and sees the stale version — looks like a bug; worse for things like rate-limit counters that depend on read-your-own-writes.

**NFR5 — Durability stance: this is a cache, not a system of record — losing recent writes on a hard crash is acceptable (RPO seconds), because the DB behind us can refill.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** "If a machine dies and we lose its last second of writes, that's OK — the real data lives in the database; the cache just refills."
- 💥 **What breaks without it (if we over-engineer durability):** forcing fsync-on-every-write would tank our latency to disk-speed and defeat the purpose of an in-memory cache.

> ⚠️ **Gap I'd flag out loud:** "True read-your-own-writes across an async replica is not free — if a client writes to the primary then a load-balanced read lands on a lagging replica, it can read stale. I'll handle it with read-from-primary-after-write or a version token, and I'll call out the bounded staleness window in §7."

> 💬 "The headline tension is this: a cache is only valuable if it's faster than the thing behind it AND absorbs almost all the load. So every decision — partitioning, replication, eviction — has to preserve sub-millisecond reads while keeping the fleet available and the origin shielded."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "So at the core this is a sharded in-memory key-value store. The functional surface is tiny — get, put with a TTL, and expiry — but the system work is huge: I need to partition keys across hundreds of nodes with consistent hashing so adding a node moves almost nothing, replicate each shard so a death is invisible, evict gracefully when RAM fills, and survive the two killers of any cache — a single hot key and a stampede when a popular key expires. My non-functionals are sub-millisecond reads, four-nines availability, linear scale to ten million ops a second, and bounded staleness. Durability I deliberately keep loose — this is a cache, the database is the source of truth."

---

## 2. Clarifying Questions & Assumptions

**Q1 — "Is this a side cache the application talks to explicitly (cache-aside), or an inline/transparent cache that proxies the database?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It completely changes who owns cache-miss logic — if it's cache-aside, the *app* reads the DB on a miss and repopulates; if it's inline, *we* do, which means we own the stampede problem entirely."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If cache-aside → client library handles miss→DB→set; we're a pure KV store; stampede mitigation is client-side (single-flight in the client lib).
  - If read-through/write-through → we proxy the DB, own miss-fill and write-back; heavier but client is dumb.
- **Assumption:** Cache-aside is the dominant real-world pattern (it's how Redis/Memcached are used), so I'll design a pure KV store and put stampede defense in a smart client library — but I'll design the server hooks so read-through is addable.

**Q2 — "What's the read:write ratio and the peak aggregate throughput we're targeting?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Caches are read-heavy; if it's 90/10 I optimize replicas-for-reads and treat writes as the cheaper path. The absolute number decides shard count."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If read-heavy (90/10) → scale by adding read replicas per shard; primary handles writes only.
  - If write-heavy → replicas don't help reads; I'd shard more aggressively and consider write-coalescing.
- **Assumption:** ~10M ops/s peak, 90% reads / 10% writes. This drives ~128 shards and replica-for-reads.

**Q3 — "How skewed is the key distribution — is there a celebrity/hot-key problem?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Uniform keys are easy — just shard. But if one key takes 5% of all reads, that single key alone outpaces a whole node, and no amount of sharding helps because one key lives on one shard."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If uniform → consistent hashing alone is enough.
  - If skewed (Zipfian) → I need hot-key mitigation: replicate the hot shard, client-side caching, and detection — this becomes a headline deep dive.
- **Assumption:** Heavily skewed (Zipfian) traffic — realistic for any cache fronting popular content. The hot key is my binding bottleneck.

**Q4 — "How strong does consistency need to be — is stale data for a few milliseconds acceptable, or do we need strong consistency?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "It decides synchronous vs asynchronous replication, which is a 10x latency difference. A cache usually tolerates staleness; a distributed lock built on the cache does not."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If eventual is fine → async replication, read from replicas, fast and cheap.
  - If strong needed → synchronous replication / quorum reads, much slower.
- **Assumption:** Eventual consistency with bounded staleness (~5 ms) and read-your-own-writes via read-from-primary. It's a cache.

**Q5 — "Do we need automatic failover and elastic resharding without downtime, or is a maintenance window acceptable?"**
- 🗣️ **Why I'm asking:** "Automatic failover needs a coordinator and a quorum to avoid split-brain — real engineering. If a 5-minute window is OK, I can do manual promotion and save complexity."
- ↔️ **Design fork:**
  - If no-downtime required → cluster manager (Sentinel/Raft-based) for auto-failover + online slot migration.
  - If windows OK → manual ops, much simpler.
- **Assumption:** Automatic failover required (seconds RTO) and online resharding — this is a shared infra tier; downtime hits everyone.

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my big five. I'm going to proceed with cache-aside, ~10M ops/s at 90/10 reads, heavily skewed traffic, eventual consistency with read-your-own-writes, and automatic failover. Does that match what you had in mind, or do you want to push on any of those before I size it?"

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I computed these by writing and running `capacity.py`. Below are the rounded results. (Glosses: **QPS / ops/s** — operations per second hitting the fleet; **shard** — one slice of the keyspace owned by one primary node; **RF** — replication factor, how many copies of each shard exist; **TTL** — time-to-live, how long a key lives before auto-expiry.)

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step math) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Peak aggregate ops/s | given target | 10M ops/s | "about ten million ops a second" | overall fleet size |
| Peak reads/s | 10M x 0.90 | 9M reads/s | "nine million reads a second" | replica count (reads scale on replicas) |
| Peak writes/s | 10M x 0.10 | 1M writes/s | "a million writes a second" | primary throughput per shard |
| Per-node ceiling | conservative single-thread small GET/SET | ~100K ops/s | "a node tops out around a hundred thousand ops" | the unit we divide by |
| Primary shards | 10M ÷ 100K = 100, +30% headroom ≈ 130 → round | 128 shards | "about a hundred and twenty-eight shards" | partitioning scheme size |
| Per-shard load | 10M ÷ 128 | ~78K ops/s | "roughly eighty thousand per shard" | confirms headroom under 100K ceiling |
| Total nodes | 128 shards x 3 copies (1 primary + 2 replicas) | 384 nodes | "just under four hundred nodes" | cost + ops footprint |
| Dataset (1 copy) | 5B keys x (50+500 B) x 1.5 overhead = 825 B/entry | ~4.1 TB | "about four terabytes of hot data" | RAM sizing |
| Total RAM (x3) | 4.1 TB x 3 copies | ~12.4 TB | "around twelve terabytes of RAM" | dominant cost driver |
| RAM per node | 12.4 TB ÷ 384 | ~32 GB | "32-gig boxes" | instance type |
| **Hot key QPS** | **9M reads x 5% on one key** | **~450K QPS** | **"the hot key alone is ~450K a second"** | **THE binding bottleneck** |
| Stampede multiple | 450K reqs hit a ~5K-QPS DB key class | ~90x | "ninety times the DB can take" | single-flight + early recompute |
| Rebalance (consistent hash) | +1 node moves ~1/N = 1/128 | ~0.8% keys move | "under one percent moves" | consistent hashing vs mod-N |
| Rebalance (naive mod N) | +1 node moves (N-1)/N | ~99% keys move | "nearly everything moves" | why NOT mod-N |
| Failover RTO | 5s detect + 1s promote | ~6 s/shard | "about six seconds to fail over" | coordinator design |
| Monthly RAM cost | 12,375 GB x ~$6/GB-mo all-in | ~$75K/mo | "around seventy-five grand a month, mostly RAM" | cost optimization target |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** the **hot key at ~450K QPS** against a **~100K ops/s node ceiling** — that's a **~4–5x single-node overload that sharding cannot fix** (one key lives on one shard). **Flip threshold:** if the hottest key stays under ~80K QPS (i.e. no key exceeds ~1% of read traffic), a single replica suffices and I can skip hot-shard replication and client-side caching entirely. Above that, hot-key mitigation becomes mandatory — so it leads my deep dives.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The number that drives everything is the hot key. We're at about nine million reads a second, and with skewed traffic one celebrity key can grab five percent of that — call it 450 thousand requests a second to a single key. A node tops out around a hundred thousand. So that one key overloads its node four or five times over, and I can't shard my way out because a key lives on exactly one shard. That's my binding bottleneck, and it's why hot-key mitigation is my first deep dive. Storage is a non-issue — about twelve terabytes of RAM across the fleet, roughly seventy-five grand a month, dominated by memory, not compute."

---

## 4. Core Entities

> This is a first-draft entity list — I'll add full field definitions in §7.

**Entry** — a single key-value pair. Owns the value bytes, its TTL/expiry timestamp, and access metadata (last-access time / frequency counter) used for eviction.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Think of it like a sticky note on a board — a label, what's written on it, and a 'throw away after' date."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "How do you store the TTL without scanning everything to find expired keys?" → "Lazy expiry on access plus a background sampling expirer — we don't scan, we sample."

**Shard (Partition / Slot range)** — a contiguous slice of the hash ring / a set of hash slots. Owns a subset of all keys and maps to exactly one primary node at a time.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "Like one drawer in a giant filing cabinet — every label belongs to exactly one drawer, decided by its hash."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What decides which shard a key lands in?" → "The key's hash position on the consistent-hashing ring (we use hash slots, 16,384 of them, mapped to shards)."

**Node (Cache server)** — a physical/VM process holding one or more shards in RAM. A node is either a **primary** (accepts writes for its shards) or a **replica** (read-only copy that can be promoted).
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "One machine in the warehouse — it physically holds several drawers and can serve copies of others."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "What happens when a node dies?" → "A replica of each shard it owned gets promoted to primary by the cluster coordinator within seconds."

**Cluster Topology / Slot Map** — the authoritative mapping of {hash slot → primary node + replica set}, plus node health. Owned by the cluster coordinator; gossiped to all nodes and cached by clients.
- 🗣️ **Analogy:** "The directory map taped to the warehouse wall: which drawer is in which machine, who's the boss of each drawer right now."
- ⚠️ **Probe:** "Isn't the topology a single point of failure / central directory on every read?" → "No — clients cache the map and route directly; they only refetch on a MOVED redirect or topology-change gossip, so reads never hit a central service."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Four entities. An Entry is the key-value-plus-TTL — the sticky note. A Shard is a slice of the keyspace, a drawer. A Node is a machine holding drawers, and it's either a primary that takes writes or a replica that can be promoted. The one people underestimate is the Cluster Topology — the slot map — because that's what lets clients route directly to the right node without a central lookup on every read; clients cache it and only refresh on a redirect. I'll flesh out fields in the data model."

---

## 5. API / Interface

The wire protocol is a thin binary/RESP-style command set, but I'll express it as logical operations. The client library holds the topology and routes directly to the owning node.

### GET — read a key (satisfies FR1)

- **Method + path:** `GET /v1/keys/{key}` (logically `GET key`). GET because it's a pure read with no state change — safe and cacheable at the client.
- **Key fields:** `key: string` (the only required input). `consistency: enum {primary|replica}` *(optional, default replica)* — lets a caller force read-from-primary for read-your-own-writes. The owning node is **not** in the request — the client computes it from the cached slot map; never trust a client-supplied node id.
- **Example:**
```
// Request
GET /v1/keys/user:42:profile?consistency=replica

// Response 200 (hit)
{ "key": "user:42:profile", "value": "{...}", "ttlSeconds": 47, "node": "shard-13-replica-2" }

// Response 404 (miss)
{ "key": "user:42:profile", "found": false }
```
- **Key design decision:** a **miss is a normal 404, not an error** — the client then reads the DB and repopulates (cache-aside). The `consistency` knob is the read-your-own-writes lever.

### PUT / SET — write a key with optional TTL (satisfies FR2, FR3)

- **Method + path:** `PUT /v1/keys/{key}`. PUT because it fully replaces the value at that key — idempotent by definition (same PUT twice = same state).
- **Key fields:** `value: bytes` (the payload); `ttlSeconds: int` *(optional)* — server computes the absolute expiry = `now + ttl`; the client sends a *relative* TTL, the **server sets the absolute timestamp** so clock skew on the client can't poison expiry. `waitForReplicas: int` *(optional, default 0)* — how many replica acks to wait for (0 = fire-and-forget, fast; 1+ = safer, slower).
- **Example:**
```
// Request
PUT /v1/keys/user:42:profile
{ "value": "{...}", "ttlSeconds": 60, "waitForReplicas": 1 }

// Response 200
{ "key": "user:42:profile", "version": 7, "replicasAcked": 1, "expiresAt": "2026-06-21T10:00:60Z" }
```
- **Key design decision:** `waitForReplicas` makes the durability/latency trade-off **per-write**, not global — a rate-limit counter can demand an ack; a page-cache entry can fire-and-forget. `version` is a server-set monotonic token the client can echo on a later read for read-your-own-writes; **client never sets version or expiresAt.**

### DELETE / EXPIRE — remove or re-TTL a key (satisfies FR3)

- **Method + path:** `DELETE /v1/keys/{key}` to remove; `PATCH /v1/keys/{key}` with `{ "ttlSeconds": N }` to change TTL. DELETE removes state; PATCH partially updates (just the TTL).
- **Key fields:** `key` only for DELETE. DELETE is idempotent — deleting a missing key returns 200, not an error.
- **Example:**
```
// Request
DELETE /v1/keys/user:42:profile
// Response 200
{ "key": "user:42:profile", "deleted": true }
```
- **Key design decision:** DELETE is broadcast to replicas asynchronously like any write; a redundant delete is a no-op, which keeps client retries safe.

### Admin — topology & resharding (satisfies FR4, FR5)

- **Method + path:** `POST /v1/cluster/nodes` (add a node → triggers slot migration); `GET /v1/cluster/topology` (clients fetch/refresh the slot map). POST creates a new cluster member.
- **Security note:** **the caller is identified from a service JWT / mTLS, never a client-supplied identity.** Cluster-admin endpoints require an infra role; data-plane GET/PUT require a tenant scope. We never trust a client-supplied node id, version, or expiry timestamp — those are server-authoritative.

> 💬 "Notice the client never tells us which node to hit and never sets the version or expiry — it computes routing locally from the cached map, and the server stamps the authoritative metadata."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The data-plane API is tiny: GET a key, PUT a value with an optional TTL, DELETE or re-TTL. The two senior touches: first, the client routes itself — it caches the slot map and computes the owning node, so reads never hit a central directory. Second, every mutating call has a server-set version and a server-computed absolute expiry, and PUT lets you choose per-write how many replicas to wait for — so a rate-limit counter can demand a replica ack while a page cache fires and forgets. Admin endpoints for adding nodes and fetching topology are locked behind an infra role."

---

## 6. High-Level Design (one operation at a time)

Shared color language across all diagrams: blue = clients, green = our cache services, amber = async/replication, grey = datastores.

### 6.1 "GET a value by key"

The simplest slice, but it establishes the routing model that everything else builds on: the client finds the owning node *without* a central lookup.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smart client library | A thin library in the caller that caches the slot map and routes each key directly to its owning node | Eliminates a central router hop on every read — keeps p99 under 1 ms | Not a proxy tier in front (Twemproxy/Envoy): adds a network hop + a thing to scale and fail | Clients must hold and refresh the topology map |
| Cache node (primary/replica) | An in-memory hash map process holding shards in RAM | RAM-speed reads; that's the entire product | Not on-disk KV (RocksDB): disk is ~1000x slower, defeats the purpose | Data is volatile; RAM is expensive |
| Read replica | A read-only copy of a shard a node can serve GETs from | Scales reads horizontally per shard for hot data | Not primary-only reads: would cap read QPS at one node per shard | Replica can be a few ms stale |
| Slot map (topology) | The {slot → primary + replicas} directory, gossiped | Lets clients self-route; one source of truth for ownership | Not per-request lookup to a directory service: central read hotspot | Clients can be briefly stale → handled by MOVED redirect |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Consistent hashing / hash slots** — instead of `hash(key) % N`, we map every key onto a fixed ring of 16,384 slots, and slots are assigned to nodes. Adding a node just reassigns *some slots*, not all keys. Here it's what makes the cache elastic.
> **Slot map** — the table of which node owns which slots. Here clients cache it so they route directly and reads stay sub-millisecond.
> **MOVED redirect** — if a client routes to a node that no longer owns a slot (stale map), the node replies "that slot moved to node X." Here it's the self-correcting mechanism for stale topology.
> **Read replica** — a passive copy of a shard that serves reads. Here it multiplies read throughput for hot shards.

**Diagram — GET hot path**
```mermaid

    Client["Client app + smart lib"]
    Client -->|"1 hash key to slot"| SlotMap["Slot map cached in client"]
    Client -->|"2 GET routed to owner"| Primary["Cache primary shard 13"]
    Client -.->|"2b GET from replica"| Replica["Cache replica shard 13"]
    Primary -->|"3 value or miss"| Client

    Primary -.->|"6 MOVED if stale map"| Client

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Hash key → slot.** The client library hashes the key into one of 16,384 slots. *Why client-side:* avoids a network hop to a router. *State change:* none — pure computation. *What if the map is stale:* the slot it computes may point at the wrong node → handled in step 6.
2. **Route GET to owning node.** The client opens a connection straight to the primary (or a replica for `consistency=replica`). *Why direct:* sub-millisecond p99 demands no extra hop. *State:* read, no change. *What if the node is down:* client fails over to a replica connection and refreshes topology.
3. **Node returns value or miss.** In-memory hash lookup. *Why:* RAM lookup is ~100 ns. *State:* updates the key's last-access time for LRU. *What if miss:* returns 404.
4. **On miss, client reads the origin DB** (cache-aside). *Why client:* cache-aside puts miss logic in the caller. *State:* DB read. *What if a popular key misses at once:* stampede — deep dive §8.4.
5. **Client repopulates** via PUT with a TTL. *State:* key now cached. *What if two clients repopulate:* PUT is idempotent, last-writer-wins by version.
6. **MOVED redirect** if the client's map was stale. *State:* client refreshes its slot map and retries. *Why:* self-healing routing without a central directory.

> 💬 "The key insight: clients route themselves using a cached map, so reads are one hop to RAM — and if the map is wrong, the node tells the client where to go instead of failing."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "On a read, the client hashes the key to one of sixteen thousand slots, looks up the owning node in its locally cached slot map, and hits that node directly — one hop, RAM lookup, sub-millisecond. No central router. If the client's map is stale because we just reshuffled, the node replies MOVED with the correct node and the client refreshes and retries. On a miss it's cache-aside: the client reads the database and writes the value back with a TTL. The trade-off is clients carry the topology, but that's exactly what buys us the no-extra-hop latency."

### 6.2 "PUT a value with optional TTL"

The write path adds replication: a write isn't done until it's safely on the primary and propagating to replicas.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Async replication stream | Primary streams each write to its replicas in the background | Keeps write latency at RAM speed (no waiting on replicas by default) | Not synchronous quorum on every write: ~10x latency, kills the cache value prop | Replicas lag a few ms → bounded staleness |
| Per-write wait-for-replicas | The `waitForReplicas` knob makes some writes wait for N acks | Lets correctness-critical writes opt into durability without slowing all writes | Not global sync: one-size-fits-all is wrong for a cache | Caller must know which writes matter |
| Expiry subsystem | Lazy-on-access + background sampling expirer for TTLs | Avoids scanning millions of keys to find expired ones | Not a precise per-key timer: millions of timers is memory + CPU death | A few expired keys linger briefly until sampled |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **Async replication** — the primary acks the write immediately and ships it to replicas in the background. Here it preserves sub-millisecond writes.
> **Replica lag** — the short window where a replica hasn't yet applied a write. Here it's our staleness bound (~5 ms).
> **Lazy + sampling expiry** — we delete an expired key when someone touches it (lazy), and a background job samples random keys to catch the rest. Here it keeps expiry cheap.

**Diagram — PUT / write path**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Client["Client app + smart lib"]
    Client -->|"1 PUT routed to primary"| Primary["Cache primary shard 13"]
    Primary -->|"2 write to RAM map"| Primary
    Primary -.->|"3 stream write async"| Replica1["Replica A shard 13"]
    Primary -.->|"3 stream write async"| Replica2["Replica B shard 13"]
    Primary -->|"4 ack with version"| Client
    Replica1 -.->|"5 ack if waitForReplicas"| Primary
    Primary -.->|"6 append to AOF"| AOF[("Append-only file local")]

    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    classDef async fill:#3d2a0b,stroke:#B7791F,color:#e6edf3
    classDef store fill:#2a2f3a,stroke:#6B7280,color:#e6edf3
    class Client client
    class Primary svc
    class Replica1,Replica2 async
    class AOF store
```
↳ reuses existing: Client, Primary, Replica (from §6.1).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **PUT routed to the primary.** Writes only go to the slot's primary. *Why:* a single writer per shard avoids write conflicts. *State:* none yet. *What if primary is down:* client gets a topology refresh; a promoted replica becomes the new primary.
2. **Write to the in-memory map** and bump version. *State:* value live and readable on the primary instantly (read-your-own-writes from primary). *What if RAM full:* eviction fires (§6.3).
3. **Stream the write asynchronously to replicas.** *Why async:* keeps write latency at RAM speed. *State:* replicas converge within ~5 ms. *What if a replica is slow:* it falls behind; backpressure / re-sync if it lags too far.
4. **Ack the client with the new version** (default, no replica wait). *State:* client holds a version token for read-your-own-writes.
5. **If `waitForReplicas≥1`, wait for replica acks** before step 4. *Why:* the per-write durability knob. *What if no replica acks in time:* return a partial-durability warning; caller decides.
6. **Append to local AOF** (append-only file) for warm restart only. *Why:* a rebooted node refills fast instead of cold. *State:* on-disk log. *Trade-off:* not a durability guarantee, just a warm-start optimization.

> 💬 "Writes go to one primary per shard and replicate in the background — fast by default, with an opt-in to wait for replicas when the data actually matters."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "A PUT always goes to the slot's single primary — one writer per shard means no write conflicts. The primary updates RAM, bumps a version, and acks immediately, streaming the change to its replicas in the background. By default we don't wait — that keeps writes at RAM speed — but a caller can set wait-for-replicas to one or more when the write is correctness-critical, like a counter. TTL is server-stamped as an absolute time so client clock skew can't poison it, and expiry is lazy plus background-sampled so we never scan the whole keyspace."

### 6.3 "Expiry / eviction (TTL + LRU/LFU)"

This slice keeps a node from ever overflowing RAM — the difference between graceful degradation and a crash.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Approximate LRU/LFU eviction | When RAM hits a watermark, evict least-recently/frequently-used keys, sampled not exact | Exact LRU needs a global linked list touched on every access — too costly at 10M ops/s | Not exact LRU: per-access list maintenance halves throughput | Evicts a near-optimal, not perfect, victim |
| Maxmemory + policy | A RAM ceiling per node and a policy (allkeys-lru, volatile-ttl, noeviction) | Bounds memory so a node degrades, never OOM-crashes | Not unbounded: OOM kills the node and all its shards | Some keys evicted earlier than ideal |

> **🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**
> **LRU (Least Recently Used)** — evict the key untouched longest. Here it's the default victim-picking rule.
> **LFU (Least Frequently Used)** — evict the key accessed fewest times. Here it's better when access frequency matters more than recency.
> **Eviction watermark / maxmemory** — the RAM line at which eviction starts. Here it's the safety valve that prevents OOM.

**Diagram — eviction**
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Write["Incoming PUT"]
    Write -->|"1 check maxmemory"| Check{"RAM over watermark"}
    Check -->|"2 no store key"| Store["Store in RAM map"]
    Check -->|"3 yes sample N keys"| Sample["Sample candidate keys"]
    Sample -->|"4 pick LRU or LFU victim"| Evict["Evict victim key"]
    Evict -->|"5 free RAM then store"| Store
    classDef svc fill:#0b3d2e,stroke:#2ea043,color:#e6edf3
    class Write,Store,Sample,Evict svc
    class Check svc
```
↳ reuses existing: cache node internals (from §6.1/6.2).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **On each write, check maxmemory.** *Why:* eviction is write-triggered, not a background sweep, so RAM is bounded exactly. *State:* none if under.
2. **Under watermark → just store.** Normal path.
3. **Over watermark → sample N random keys** (e.g. 5). *Why sampling:* approximating LRU/LFU over a sample avoids maintaining a global ordering on the hot path. *State:* none yet.
4. **Pick the LRU/LFU victim** from the sample. *State:* victim chosen.
5. **Evict and free RAM, then store the new key.** *State:* node stays under its ceiling. *What if a hot key gets evicted:* the sampling rarely picks hot keys (they're recently/frequently used), and client-side caching shields them anyway.

> 💬 "Eviction is approximate-LRU on a small random sample — we never maintain a perfect ordering because at ten million ops a second that bookkeeping would itself be the bottleneck."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Eviction is what keeps a node from ever crashing on memory. We set a max-memory ceiling, and when a write would push past it, the node samples a handful of random keys and evicts the least-recently or least-frequently used among them. It's approximate on purpose — maintaining an exact LRU list would mean touching a global structure on every single access, which at our throughput would halve performance. The trade-off is we occasionally evict a slightly-sub-optimal key, which for a cache is completely fine."
